turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next in Carter v. Kentucky. Mr. McNally, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case comes here from Hopkinsville, Kentucky, states passed a statute permitting the defendant to testify and stating that no comment shall be made.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I'm addressing myself to, the prohibition against comments.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: The instruction itself does not speci­ fically address the issue of a protective instruction. The Kentucky Supreme Court has on a number of occasions inter­ preted that statute to mean that no comments at all may be permitted regarding the defendant's failure to testify.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the question here that you're bringing to us is not whether this would be a sensible and a sound thing to do but that the Constitution requires that the we think, a reasonable interpretation of the statute. But we do not bring here a question of state interpretation. And as the Chief Justice pointed out, this is not a case of what is the most desirable rule, although our position is, we submit, by light of the vast majority of authorities, the most desir­ able rule. The question here is, as referred to in Cupp v. Naughten, what exactly does the Constitution specifically re­ quire? And that's what makes this case different from any other alleged instructional error. We have no intention of dwelling on the facts of this case but there are two principal factual themes we would like to emphasize. What they do is dramatize legal principles which perhaps in another case we might be arguing in the abstract. The first of those is what we would call the "roar" of the defendant's silence at trial. There was a continuing theme throughout this trial that the defendant refused to explain himself at any point in the proceedings. He refused to explain himself to the police, and this was testified to by

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's on the alternative; I see.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: In the alternative. Of course, our primary reliance is on the facts of this case. However, would we not have these, what I would call, illustrative facts in this case, of the legal principles, we would still be making the same arguments.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, now, while I have you interrupted, may I ask, and do you also take the position that if the trial to do anything. Now, while that is constitutionally accurate, we submit that it may have been perceived as either inaccurate by the jury or perhaps even arrogande on the part Of defense counsel. This illustrates why we need the court's protection. There is no effective way a trial lawyer can deal with this issue, at least under the circumstances of this case, other than in instruction from the court. For example, he couldn't

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I still don't understand why the reason is of interest to us?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Merely because it illustrates, Mr. Justice Marshall, the point that has been made in perhaps the abstract in other cases, that there are neutral reasons why a defendant would decide not to testify at trial. It is illustrative, ittis not necessary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does the Fifth Amendment say anything about that?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: No, it doesn't, Mr. Justice Marshall. that? concern in due process analysis has been the truth-seeking function, and we submit that under the facts of this case the jury --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how do you get truth by'a..man not testifying?,

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: The point we are trying to make, Mr. Justice Marshall, is that the jury naturally and we think

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I withdraw the question because I'm holding you up.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: -- concluded that -- well, I think it's an important question. The jury concluded that the of it?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: We frankly -- frankly, we disagree with the argument that a defendant in some cases will not notice, the jury will not notice that a defendant does not testify. However, that is not:essential to this particular case and again, we ar'e not suggesting that Lakeside was wrongly decided. Indeed, we put great reliance on the ration­ ale of this Court in Lakeside.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. McNally, I gather then the argument you're now making is largely a due process argument, in the favor of the defendant. familiar with the precise state of the legislation in the allowed to testify or he was allowed to testify? finds great support in prior decisions of this Court. In Wilson v. United States, this Court first held that comment, adverse comment was prohibited. It did so under a federal statute and not under the Fifth Amendment. In Bruno v. United States shortly thereafter this Court dealt with the exact issue presented here and ruled, not as a matter of federal constitutional law, but pursuant to the federal statute, that an instruction was required. An unanimous Court decided that. Subsequently, in Malloy v. Hogan, this Court held the Fifth Amendment applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. Immediately after that the Court dealt with the issue presented in Wilson in Griffin v. California. And the Court stated at that time that the significant form of compulsion -- and in that sense, this to the accused. But nevertheless, if it must be given on request, because the Fifth Amendment requires it, why must it not be given just as a matter of course?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, this Court has held in cases such as Wainwright v. Sykes, for example, that trial counsel can waive issues of federal constitutional negligence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If he doesn't request it, it is to be treated as if he waived it, is that it?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: That would be our position,although that is again not presented --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That would be an intelligent waiver.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: It may be under the facts --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how could it be an intelligent waiver if it wasn't mentioned?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, it may be a tactical waiver. Whether it's intelligent or not, Mr. Justice Marshall, we'd have to examine the particular case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it may be the reason for no request may be more than a waiver, it may be an affirmative reason as, by the reasoning of the dissenting opinion in Lakeside.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: If trial counsel waives his right to this instruction?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: As a matter of tactics.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, as defense counsel in Lakeside did exactly that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Exactly. And there was a dissenting opinion, joined in part by Mr. Justice Marshall, that agreed with the reasoning of defense counsel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that make it great?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: We would like to turn,"if the Court permits --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Dissenting.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: -- once again to our Fourteenth Amendment analysis and make some --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could you tell me, do the counsel in Kentucky argue before or after the judge instructs?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: The counsel argues after the judge instructs, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And did you try to get him to join you?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: I did not, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Trial counsel did suggest to the jury that the defendant wasn't required to take the stand --

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: He did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and that -- now, doesn't that bear on your due process argument? The prosecutor didn't say to the contrary. And the judge certainly didn't indicate to the contrary.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, we would disagree on a few points, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I only made one. MR. McNALLY-: We would disagree on your general point to the extent that the prosecutor did not take any advantage or did not denigrate the particular right; to that extent. Secondly, I think this Court recognized in Taylor v. Kentucky that while counsel's arguments may be relevant in the overall due process analysis, counsel's instructions are not a -- I'm sorry -- counsel's arguments are not a sub­ stitute for the court's instructions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, In Kentucky, I take it, that coun­ sel can tell a jury what he thinks the law is, even though the judge hasn't instructed on it?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: I don't believe that's the law of our jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he just did, this counsel did.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: He did it in contravention of Kentucky law and he got away with it without objection from the prosecutor. And I think the reason for that may answer your question. The reason why he got away with it is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I just bring it up because it does bear on your due process argument in this particular case. Mr. Justice White, and I would submit that the reason why been in the eyes Of the jury hurting himself.. his statement of the law may be dismissed as either inaccu­ rate, as we mentioned before, or just simply arrogant, stating that the defendant doesn't have to do anything, may not have sat well with the jury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if you get away from the facts of this particular case, then the question is simply the one reserved in Griffin, I take it? Whether, in every case, re­ gardless of the .factual setting or anything else, an accused is entitled to an instruction that his silence need not be considered, if he requests such an instruction?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, we think that issue, the per se rule, if you will, that's presented in this case -- we ar­ gued it below and it's well presented -- the Court may not choose to reach that, but we think it is framed in this case, Mr. Justice Rehnquist.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it was explicitly left open in the Griffin case, wasn't it?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: I believe that also was mentioned in Lakeside, Mr. Justice Stewart.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if a state's decided that the instruction about the burden of proof and the presumption of innocence was so basic, so fundamental to the whole proceed­ ing, that it required that before any evidence was received the judge give the certain general Instruction explaining both, in every case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Before any evidence; and would ex­ plain it, as a good many judges do now, in both federal and state courts, on the theory that then the jury will better understand the evidence as it comes in, and they will not be sitting around for two or three days wondering what the defendant is going to say in answer to these charges.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I don't know that the Due Process Clause goes to the timing of a particular instruction. I think any petitioner would have to be satis­ the timing of that particular instruction would be an issue of constitutional magnitude. If I may address myself to just a few points in terms of a due process analysis, again, number one, this instruction deals with a specific constitutional right. Number two, this Court has recognized that a defendant's tes­ timony is expected, even in a case that doesn't present the facts that we have here. Number three, instructions do work, and it's basically a pillar of our criminal justice system. Instructions are effective, and this Court recog­ nized that, of course, in Lakeside. Number four, silence is ambiguous. Again, if you take away the facts of this case, silence, as this Court noted in Doyle v. Ohio, pretrial is ambiguous; it is even more so at trial, Insolubly ambiguous. because there may be reasons why a defendant such as Mr. Carter declines to testify, unrelated to guilt or innocence. And the final two points are that, while it's not dispositive of the due process issue presented, we find widespread support in the overwhelming number of jurisdic­ tions. Our research has only disclosed three, or perhaps four, jurisdictions that don't give this instruction upon request. And finally, the last criterion is that state interest. And I would ask the Court to examine what important terest in the Commonwealth's brief is that the instruction should be prohibited in the defendant's own interest, and we submit that the Commonwealth stating that it's in the defen­ dant's interest not to get a protective instruction is not the kind of basic exercise of a state's jurisdiction which need be given due -- perhaps it needs to be given due deference but not undue deference in any Fourteenth Amendment analysis. And unless the Court has questions, I will re­ serve --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I have just one question. You've said several times that you think the Lakeside case helps you. I'm not sure I understand why you think it helps you.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, the holding of it is really neutral on the question presented in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe it's neutral, but you seem to think it helped you.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Well, what I was referring to was the language, and specifically on two points: the first being that juries will notice a defendant's failure to testi­ fy, even if in that case defense counsel takes great pains to avoid any mention of the issue. We think that is a sig­ nificant point in this case. And the second point being that protective instructions are effective. There was lan­ guage in the majority opinion which addressed itself to those two, and we think they are important considerations for our due process analysis. To that extent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Also, the definition of that word comment in Lakeside helps you, I think.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Again, Mr. Justice Stewart, we agree. We'll reserve the remainder of our time for rebuttal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Mr. Bullock.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The facts in this case point overwhelmingly to the guilt of the petitioner, in this particular instance. If you will recall, Officer Ellison was driving her cruiser down the street, saw an alley, looked in the alley and saw something suspicious. She backed up, shined her light down the alley, saw two individuals crouched. They took off running, She went down the alley and saw that there was a hole in the side of Young's Hardware Store. She gave out a call on her police radio for Officer Davis who was in the vicinity. He saw petitioner running down an alley; he gave chase. They appar­ ently ran parallel for a period of time, at which time Officer Davis cut across and almost ran into the petitioner. When Officer Ellison saw him, saw the individual at the hardware store, he was carrying a gym bag, what she thing, sort of orange pants, and so forth. He was apprehended by Officer Davis --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How does this, counsel, how does this bear on the issue that's presented here?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: I believe two-fold, Your Honor. Williams? ; ■ '. , •

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes, sir. I understand that position. The second issue, if it please the Court, is that it would appear to us that there was very little likelihood under any circumstances that the defendant in this particular case would have taken the stand. As such, we are suggesting that this in effect may be a manufactured type constitutional issue, be­ cause the facts were so overwhelming. It would be very un­ likely for any defense counsel to recommend to his client that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Couldn't that easily have been solved by

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, what are the facts of interest to us?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: The point, I guess, I was/pointing to, Mr. Justice Marshall, is that this was a foregone conclu­ sion and in effect the constitutional issue itself was manu­ factured because they knew that he wasn't going to be able to take the stand in a case like this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Isn't the constitutional issue the only issue before us?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Well, there's two constitutional issues according to my brother, Mr. McNally.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think we took the case to decide a harmless error question?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: To find a Fifth Amendment violation in this particular case, there must be a finding of compul­ sion under the Fifth Amendment cases. We would agree that there is a difference in some of the cases which indicate, the trilogy of cases, Bruno, Griffin, and Lakeside, which in­ dicate maybe a preference for this type of an instruction. The Supreme Court of Kentucky, as you've noted, does not be­ lieve that the instruction should be given, and in fact they have stated on numerous occasions that they believe it does more harm than good. On the other hand, as we've noted, there is some indication that this Court would prefer the instruction. As has been pointed out many times by the Court, differences or incongruity within the limits of fundamental fairness is at the heart of our federal systems. The formulation of procedural rules to govern the administration of criminal justice in the various states is properly a matter of local concern. Of course, that's always subject to the parameters of the United States Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you suggesting Kentucky is kind of a lean instruction state where the preference within consti­ tutional limits, not just on this particular kind of a charge but on any charge, is that you just don't charge the jury at trial?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Well, that has been stated in at least one Kentucky case, but the State -- my point is, the State of Kentucky can be different, if it chooses to be, as long as it falls within the parameters of the United States Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that parameters or perimeters?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: I thought it was parameters. Maybe it's perimeters. In discussing compulsion -- and this Court has Rooked at cases similar to this, but, this issue -- the defendant in this case did not testify. We suggested why he probably did not testify. But since he did not testi­ fy, there was in fact no compulsion to testify under the Fifth Amendment. The procedures that were used were the normal procedures of a trial, such as required for a decision on the defendant's part. He has to decide in a case like that whether he wants to testify or not to testify, and this is the normal state of trial procedure. It is not compulsion by the State of Kentucky in a situation like this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you think that knowing in advance what kind of instruction the judge would give one way or the other would have any influence on his decision?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: It might.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then aren't you admitting that there is some compulsion, if there's no instruction when he wants it?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: I don't believe so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if you say that the fact the judge is not going to give the instruction may influence him not to testify, then isn't that a factor that may tend to compel him not to testify?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: But, sir, this is not a compulsion by the State of Kentucky. This is the normal trial situation, as opposed to something that the State has compelled upon the defendant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the State has a rule that the judge can't give this kind of instruction. That rule, it seems to me you have just suggested, does have an impact on his decision.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes. The Court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you're saying it is not a form of compulsion.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Well, the court has held in the past that it's not a form of compulsion for a defendant to have to make hard choices in the case, such as, the Court has held that when an individual decides to take the stand and testify examination as to the guilt on that particular case. And that's not considered compulsion. Likewise, the Court in a single verdict death penalty case has said, even though a man may have to plead for his life, his very life, the fact that if he gets on the stand and may be cross-examined, this is not considered compulsion; McGautha case. The supposition that a failure of a particular defendant to testify is a supposition, it's not a proven fact as such. No one knows what an individual jury will think or will not think.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would you imagine a jury would think, other than that the man is guilty?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: I think that they may well not notice at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they're not, they wouldn't think anything of it?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: They may not notice at all, or dis­ cuss it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They may not notice it?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: In -- that's been part of the supposi­ tion of the Kentucky courts on this particular issue. But there is an additional part of the instruction in this particular case, if you will recall. The court in this case instructed that the jury shall consider the evidence alone and in arriving at its decision on guilt or innocence, the failure to testify is obviously not evidence, and its con­ sideration would have violated the court's instruction. Therefore --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask just one more question.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you want me to assume that there is a possibility that throughout the jury proceedings not one of the 12 will mention the fact that he didn't take the standi

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: That there is a possibility?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is a possibility that that will not happen?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: I'm sorry, maybe I misunderstood

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You said originally that you could assume that nobody noticed it. Am I to assume that not one of the 12 noticed it?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: In a given case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: There could be such a case; yes, sir,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Go ahead.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: We would take the position that to reverse this particular case on the no inference instruction Amendment in an unprecedented manner and would in effect give an inequitable result. Neither the Fifth or Fourteenth Amendments cause a penalty, since the Court has created no undue or ad­ ditional pressure upon the defendant to take the stand. Any of the consequences of the silence are defendant's choice, a part of trial tactics and not caused by the courts or the statutes of the State of Kentucky. If we may, let me draw an analogy between this and the situation in Taylor v. Kentucky and Kentucky v. Wharton. In those two court cases, this Court held that under certain factual patterns where the evidence was not overwhelming, there may be a totality of circumstances that constitutionally require a presumption of innocence instruction in that case. The Court In Wharton, however, pointed out that there is not tion of innocence instruction. We feel that in this particu­ lar case there is no constitutional requirement to give the no inference instruction. The two types of instruc­ tions are somewhat similar. It has been attacked in the petitioner's brief, or it has been stated in the petitioner's brief, that the State of Kentucky has not been consistent, has waffled back and forth on the question of whether it's best to give the no inference instruction. consistently maintained that no inference instruction is not in the best interests of an accused. However, in one case, at least, lit by dicta said that the instruction under those circumstances might have been allowed. It was later repudiated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, hasn't the deviation by the Kentucky court been on the construction of the meaning of the Kentucky statute?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What the no comment rule means?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so the Kentucky court has not been completely consistent about that?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: That's correct; yes. At first it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, that's the present law?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: That is the present law, yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would be that the statute means that this instruction must not be given?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: This instruction must not be given.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's the present law of Kentucky, isn't it?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the law is that no one would comment, what about the defendant's own lawyer explaining to the jury that there is a Fifth Amendment privilege, and so forth?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: He made that, and in this particular -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know he made it, but your adversary indicated that he did it illegally.

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: Yes. We pointed that out in our brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your view is that even his own lawyer in Kentucky is not permitted to?

turns.name: ROBERT V. BULLOCK
turns.text: That's what case law in Kentucky says; yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would the question arise in the case where a guilty verdict is returned? Who is there to complain about it, then, if there's no appeal? The problem is academic then, isn't it? various states as to their holdings on the no inference in­ struction. We do not specifically disagree as to each of these cases in our brief. The reason is that it would have caused a brief in and of itself. We would point out that there is room for difference of opinion as to the holding of the various states, and it would be our position, more im­ portantly, that it really isn't germane in and of itself, since conceivably there could be 49 states that require the no inference instruction and Kentucky be the lone state the other way. But as long as there was no constitutional violation, Kentucky would be within its rights as a separate state. The petitioner in this case received a fair trial. One of the questions earlier was whether a judge could explain various rights during a voir dire. That's exactly what happened in this particular case. The defendant's rights were explained to the jury. It was stated that he was presumed by the law to be innocent. The judge took great pains to explain and make sure that there was a fair trial during the voir dire. And, as I mentioned, there was an instruction that they could find the defendant guilty only from the evidence alone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is your view on Lakeside v. Oregon? of it, that the option is the defendant's. wise, must be safe and secure with the knowledge that the perpetrators of crime will be punished if they're guilty. Constitutional grounds for reversal should not be manufacturer or decided on tenuous or speculative grounds. In this case it is our position there is neither a Fifth nor a Fourteenth Amendment violation of law. If there are no further questions, I will --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Do you have anything further, Mr. McNally?

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Just a few points, if I may.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: I'd just like to address ourselves briefly to the question of harmless error since we didn't originally address that. First of all, it was not presented below, and after dictional but we think that there are good reasons why this Court would not want to reach that particular issue at this time. Since Kentucky is one of the few jurisdictions with jury sentencing, we feel that any harmless error analysis would have to include Kentucky's unique procedure and in that sense the policy is correct, that it would be better to let the Kentucky Supreme Court address that issue first. But secondly, if the Court were to address it, Bruno v. United States as I understand it is one of the leading federal cases on harmless error, and again, a unanimous Court rejected the contention that the refusal to protect the construction is harmless error in that case.

turns.name: KEVIN MICHAEL MCNALLY
turns.text: Substantial rights, I believe, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, is the key phrase in the statute you refer to. And we submit that the Court's holding in Bruno that the substantial rights of the defendant were affected without any factual analysis in the opinion indicates that in this case it also affected the substantial rights ; and fur­ ther, that the Chapman and Harrington analysis is a stricter standard from our perspective than even the federal harmless error statute, and in that sense, since it's not harmless error in federal court, it couldn't possibly be harmless error once this court addresses, if it does, the question in federal constitutional terms. And finally, the evidence, we submit, is not over­ whelming in a constitutional sense, and indeed, we admit that there are substantial incriminating circumstances under which the defendant was arrested. One other reason why a harmless error analysis wouldn't be practical in this situa­ tion is, is,that the stronger the incriminating circumstances the stronger the desire of the jury is going to be to hear the defendant testify in the first place. And in that sense we submit that harmless error analysis would be inappropriate, as to this question. Finally, I might address myself to the statement that the issue was somehow manufactured by trial counsel. We submit that, first of all, the Commonwealth conceded testify because of his fear of impeachment. But even, as Mr. Justice Marshall pointed out, even if those facts weren't merely illustrative of the legal point that we were trying CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 79-5060 LONNIE JOE CARTER V. COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY proceedings for the records of the Court. BY:
